exemplar.title: An experiment studying the effect of lyrical and non-lyrical music on word recall

detailedExemplarData.title: An experiment studying the effect of lyrical and non-lyrical music on word recall

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear and focused aim with explicit realâworld relevance
Accurate description of the Working Memory Model and its link to the investigation
Wellâoperationalised independent and dependent variables in the hypothesis
weaknesses: Lacks specification of statistical significance threshold in the hypotheses
Theory section could include more recent citations for depth
Relevance explanation is concise but could be more detailed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The introduction demonstrates a clear aim, solid theoretical grounding and fully operationalised hypotheses. Two strands comfortably meet topâband descriptors (aim/theory), while one strand is exemplary. Minor omissions (e.g. p-value threshold, fuller citations) prevent a perfect 6, so a holistic score of 5 accurately reflects strong performance with room for refinement.

strengths: Independentâmeasures design is well explained with reference to order effects and demand characteristics
Participant inclusion criteria are clearly justified, enhancing internal validity
Controlled variables and materials are justified to prevent confounds
weaknesses: Opportunity sampling justification omits discussion of representativeness
Randomisation procedure lacks detail on bias checks
Justification for acoustic matching of materials is not fully explained
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration section consistently meets the top descriptors: research design, variable control and materials are all explained rather than merely described. Sampling is only partially linked to the aim, and some procedural details need elaboration, but overall the methodology is sound and merits the full 4 marks.

strengths: Appropriate use of descriptive and inferential statistics with clear justification
Graph correctly addresses the hypothesis and is labeled accurately
Findings are interpreted in relation to data and connected to the Working Memory Model
weaknesses: Minor discrepancy in the quoted critical value for the MannâWhitney U test
Graph omits error bars, limiting insight into variability
Incorrect notation of degrees of freedom for a nonparametric test
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Statistical analysis, graphical presentation and interpretation all satisfy highâband criteria, demonstrating sound technique and correct linkage to theory. However, calculation and reporting errors (critical value, df notation) and missing error bars indicate inaccuracies that restrict the section to 4 out of 6 rather than the top band.

strengths: Discussion of findings references both the Quinn & McConnell study and the Working Memory Model
Strengths and limitations are relevant to validity and generalisability
Modifications are explicitly linked to identified limitations and justified
weaknesses: Discussion does not propose strategies to mitigate low ecological validity
Some redundancy in the evaluation section reduces conciseness
Depth of analytical insight could be expanded, especially for fieldâbased suggestions
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation effectively links results back to theory, articulates pertinent strengths and limitations, and proposes justified modifications. The failure to address ecological validity mitigation and occasional repetition mean it meets but does not exceed the 3â4 band across strands, warranting 4 out of 6.

strandSelections.reasoning: The bar graph is correctly labelled, depicts the means for each condition, and directly addresses the hypothesis. While visually simple, it contains no major errors and therefore fits the top band; a score of 5 (not 6) recognises limited detail such as absence of error bars.

The student links the statistical outcome to acceptance of the experimental hypothesis and ties this to the WMM. Interpretation is clear, correct, and data-based, fulfilling the top band. Depth of theoretical discussion remains moderate, so a 5 is assigned.

The hypothesis correctly identifies and operationalises the IV (type of music: lyrical vs non-lyrical) and the DV (number of correctly recalled words within a defined time). Both null and alternative hypotheses are provided in fully operational terms, meeting the top-band requirement. The clarity and precision merit the upper mark of 6.

Opportunity sampling is described and some justification is offered (time constraints, pre-formed groups, language control). However, the choice is only partially linked to the aim; issues such as representativeness are touched on briefly. This meets the lower end of the âexplainedâ band, so a 3 is given.

The Working Memory Model is outlined with its components and the role of the phonological loop. The student then links this mechanism directly to the predicted overload created by simultaneous music and word presentation. The description is accurate and the connection to the current investigation is explicit, satisfying the highest band. As the description is sound but not exceptionally detailed (e.g., lacks citations of later WMM research), a score of 5 is appropriate.

Findings are compared with the original Quinn & McConnell study and interpreted through the WMM, demonstrating discussion rather than mere description. The treatment is adequate but not deeply analytical, placing it comfortably at the top of the 3-4 band.

The student states a clear and focused aim â to determine whether lyrical or non-lyrical music affects word recall â and then explicitly explains its practical relevance for students who frequently study with background music. This explicit link between the investigation and a real-world context fulfils the descriptor for the top band. Minor brevity in the relevance section keeps the explanation from being exceptionally thorough, so the strand is awarded the lower mark of the 5-6 range.

Multiple strengths (independent design, control, simplicity) and limitations (low ecological validity, convenience sample) are stated and explained with relevance to validity and generalisability, matching the 5-6 descriptor. Explanation is solid but not exhaustive, so 5 is appropriate.

Key controlled variables (same song, timing, environment) and the materials (scripts, recordings, consent forms) are presented with rationale showing how they prevent confounds. This constitutes an explanation rather than mere description, justifying the top mark (4) within the 3-4 band.

Proposed modifications (matched-pairs allocation, excluding psychology students, adding a no-music control) are explicitly linked to identified limitations and justified. This meets the criteria for the top band; depth is adequate but not exceptional, meriting a score of 5.

The design (independent measures) is not only described but also explained with reference to order effects and demand characteristics, satisfying the âexplainedâ descriptor. The explanation is concise yet explicit, so the top of the 3-4 band (4) is awarded.

Appropriate descriptive (mean, SD) and inferential (Mann-Whitney U) statistics are applied. Calculations are shown and the test choice is justified. A minor discrepancy in the quoted critical value suggests slight inaccuracy, so the strand is awarded 5 rather than 6.

annotations.comment: The student concisely explains the aim and links it to real-world relevance (students studying with background noise), fulfilling the requirement for a clearly stated aim with explained relevance.
The opening definition of cognitive psychology is verbose and general. A more focused description that directly sets up the WMM would streamline the introduction.
Turner (2008) is cited in the model description but lacks a full reference. Adding a proper citation would strengthen the theoretical foundation.
Independent and dependent variables are operationalized clearly with specific item count and time limits, demonstrating precision in hypothesis formulation.
Neither the null nor alternative hypothesis specifies the statistical significance threshold (e.g., p < 0.05). Explicitly stating this would clarify the testing criteria.
Rephrase the null hypothesis to directly contrast the expected direction of the alternative; this improves clarity in statistical testing.
The rationale for using an independent measures design is well explained with reference to demand characteristics and order effects, showing strong methodological understanding.
The control variables section blends description and justification. Separating what was controlled from why it was controlled would improve clarity.
Explain how the coin flip procedure meets standard randomization protocols and detail any checks for bias.
How was experimenter bias minimized? Consider describing any blinding procedures employed.
Participant inclusion criteria are clearly stated (age, language, hearing ability), enhancing internal validity by controlling confounds.
Opportunity sampling justification mentions time constraints but omits discussion of representativeness; address how this affects generalizability.
Consider stratified sampling to balance key demographics (e.g., gender, year group) which would strengthen external validity.
Recording materials are listed but the matching of audio volume and tempo between conditions is not justified; detail how acoustic equivalence was ensured.
The procedure is precisely detailed with timing controls and sequence, which supports replicability and internal validity.
Scoring rules exclude spelling errors but do not define treatment of synonyms beyond homophones; clarify this to ensure consistency.
Descriptive (mean, SD) and MannâWhitney U test are appropriate for ordinal data and non-normal distribution choice.
The bar graph addresses the hypothesis but omits error bars, which would illustrate variability and support interpretation.
Include an effect size (e.g., r) alongside p-values to indicate the magnitude of the observed difference.
The report of degrees of freedom for the MannâWhitney U is incorrect; U tests are nonparametric and do not use df notation.
The additional paragraph on SD and outliers repeats previous interpretation without new insight; consider merging or elaborating to avoid redundancy.
Strengths section clearly links the independent design control to increased internal validity, demonstrating relevance to the hypothesis.
The discussion of low ecological validity identifies a limitation but lacks proposed strategies to mitigate this issue in future studies.
Propose a field study component or naturalistic observation design to enhance ecological validity and address this limitation.
Proposed modifications are directly linked to identified sample and design limitations, showing strong justification for matched-pair allocation and no-music control.
Cite the source for the critical U-value. Providing the table reference strengthens transparency in statistical reporting.
Adding a control condition with ambient noise rather than music would isolate music-specific cognitive effects and strengthen causal claims.
Several references (Dhotre & Mauve) are self-published and lack peer review. Including more peer-reviewed studies on auditory distraction would enhance credibility.
Augment references with recent peer-reviewed articles on the Working Memory Model and auditory distraction to support theoretical claims.
